summary: My name is Ken Burchell, and I've been a pilot sergeant with the U.S. Park Police Special Forces Branch in Washington, D.C. for over sixteen years. On September 11, 2001, I was involved in training officers for riot control when the attacks happened, and I quickly shifted into emergency response mode. I helped coordinate launching aircraft for medevac missions and worked closely with my fellow officers and medics during the crisis. It was a day that tested all of our training and resolve.

I serve as a pilot sergeant with the U.S. Park Police Special Forces Branch, specifically in the Aviation Section here in Washington, D.C. On September 11, 2001, the U.S. Park Police played a critical role in responding to the attacks, launching aircraft to medevac injured personnel and assist in rescue operations. We had been preparing for World Bank and IMF demonstrations just days before, so tensions were already high. Our training and coordination with military medics proved invaluable during the crisis.

I have been stationed with the U.S. Park Police Aviation Section here in Washington, D.C. for over sixteen years. On the morning of September 11, 2001, while preparing for upcoming World Bank and IMF demonstrations, we quickly shifted to emergency response after learning about the attacks on the World Trade Center. Washington, D.C. was on high alert, and we coordinated closely with other agencies to respond to the unfolding crisis. The proximity to the Pentagon and National Airport made the situation even more intense.

On the morning of September 11, 2001, I was training officers in riot control when my wife alerted us to the first plane hitting the World Trade Center. Initially, some thought it might be an accident like the Empire State Building crash during World War II, but seeing the clear skies and the second plane hit made it clear this was a deliberate attack. We quickly mobilized, launched aircraft for medevac missions, and responded to the Pentagon crash site near Washington, D.C. It was a chaotic and intense day that required all our training and focus.

In early September 2001, we were preparing for a second round of demonstrations against the World Bank scheduled for about a week after the attacks. Our unit was already conducting riot training to prepare officers for potential unrest during these protests. The tension was palpable even before September 11, and the events of that day shifted our focus dramatically from protest preparation to emergency response. The World Bank demonstrations were a significant part of our planning at the time.

Alongside the World Bank, the International Monetary Fund was also the focus of planned demonstrations in Washington, D.C. around the time of September 11, 2001. Our unit was conducting riot training to prepare for these events, which heightened the sense of alertness in the days leading up to the attacks. The IMF protests were part of the broader context in which we were operating, and the sudden shift to emergency response after the attacks was dramatic. We had to quickly repurpose our training efforts to deal with the crisis.

On the morning of September 11, 2001, I first learned about the attack on the World Trade Center when my wife called and told John Marsh to turn on the television. Watching the first plane hit the towers was surreal, and when the second plane crashed in, it became clear this was a coordinated attack. The images of the World Trade Center burning under a clear blue sky made it obvious this was no accident. The attacks set off a chain of emergency responses that involved our aviation unit and medevac missions.

When the first plane hit the World Trade Center on September 11, 2001, some of us initially thought it might be similar to the incident during World War II when a bomber accidentally crashed into the Empire State Building. That event was an accident caused by bad weather and pilot error, with limited loss of life. However, seeing the clear skies on 9/11 and the deliberate nature of the attacks made it clear this was something very different. The Empire State Building crash was a point of comparison but did not reflect the severity of the attacks that day.

On September 11, 2001, after the Pentagon was hit, I could see from Washington, D.C. a fireball and smoke rising over Virginia. The crash phone from National Airport alerted us to the incident, and we quickly mobilized to respond. We launched aircraft to assist with medevac missions, some of which involved transporting injured people to hospitals in Virginia and other nearby locations. Virginia was a critical area in the response due to its proximity to the Pentagon and National Airport.

On the morning of September 11, 2001, the crash phone from National Airport sounded an alarm that alerted us to the unfolding crisis. This phone is a direct hotline used for emergencies and tests every morning at 9:00. The proximity of National Airport to the Pentagon and Washington, D.C. made it a key communication point during the attacks. We relied on this line to coordinate our aviation response and launch medevac flights.

John Marsh was one of the rescue technicians with us on September 11, 2001. My wife called and told him to turn on the television, and he was among the first to see the attacks on the World Trade Center. He was part of the duty crew that quickly responded to the emergency, running out to prepare for launch. John was integral to our team’s rapid mobilization that day.

Keith Bohn is one of the other pilots with the U.S. Park Police Aviation Section. On September 11, 2001, I saw him coming out of the hangar, and we quickly decided to take the second aircraft together. Keith started the aircraft while Tom Greer worked on installing the mass casualty kit. We launched with a full crew including the unit commander and Sergeant Bernie Stasulli to respond to the emergency. Keith was essential in getting the aircraft airborne quickly for the medevac mission.

Our Aviation Section has had a longstanding program with Department of Defense medics for over a decade. These military medics train with us and provide critical medical support during emergencies. On September 11, 2001, two of these DOD medics were with us in the field, and I ran to get them to join the flight crew for the medevac missions. This partnership with the Department of Defense has been a source of pride and vital capability for our operations.

Tom Greer is our maintenance supervisor and the go-to guy for anything mechanical. On September 11, 2001, while Keith Bohn was starting the aircraft, Tom was trying to install the mass casualty kit. Although we all wanted to help, it probably slowed him down a bit, but his expertise was crucial to getting the aircraft ready. Tom’s role was vital in ensuring the helicopter was equipped properly for the emergency medevac flights.

Sergeant Bernie Stasulli was the XO on duty with us on September 11, 2001. We loaded him and the unit commander onto the aircraft even though they weren't fully trained crew members, because we anticipated the severity of the situation. Bernie helped maintain the perimeter around the aircraft during our medevac operations at the Pentagon crash site. His presence was important for security and coordination during the mission.

Eagle One was the command aircraft given authority on scene during the Pentagon response on September 11, 2001. They directed our aircraft to land and coordinate medevac operations. We were aware of unauthorized aircraft inbound, and Eagle One helped manage the airspace and emergency response. Their leadership was critical in organizing the chaotic situation.

During the medevac operations following the Pentagon attack on September 11, 2001, we anticipated ferrying injured patients to hospitals in Baltimore. We were prepared for a large number of casualties and planned to shuttle people back and forth throughout the afternoon. Baltimore was one of the key regional medical centers involved in the emergency response. Our aviation unit was ready to make multiple trips to support the hospitals there.

Richmond, Virginia was another destination for transporting injured patients during the September 11, 2001 medevac operations. We expected to fly people with burns and other injuries to hospitals there as part of the emergency response. Richmond was part of the broader regional support network activated after the Pentagon attack. Our aviation unit was prepared to make multiple flights to Richmond to assist with casualty care.

On September 11, 2001, after the second plane hit the World Trade Center, we heard a thud and saw a fireball over Virginia, which turned out to be the Pentagon attack. We launched aircraft from Washington, D.C. to respond and landed on the west side of the highway near the Pentagon. The scene was chaotic but the personnel there were composed and purposeful, evacuating and assisting victims. We crossed into the debris field, coordinated with fire department triage officers, and began medevac operations. The Pentagon was a central focus of our emergency response that day.
